skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create AI avatar, talking head, lip sync, and dubbing videos with inference.sh CLI workflows.
skill.summary: Generate presenter-style avatar videos from portraits, scripts, audio, or translated speech.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly true positives because the skill instructs users to run belt and npx commands. The network findings are expected for an inference.sh media workflow, while the weak cryptography alert at SKILL.md line 3 is a false positive. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, destructive filesystem behavior, or arbitrary shell execution beyond documented CLI use.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Execute Remote Inference Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users to run belt app commands and npx skill installation commands. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it executes external tooling and can submit user media or scripts to remote inference apps.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous, and they call external CLI tools. The commands appear aligned with the skill purpose rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs Used for Documentation and Media Inputs
low_findings.description: The skill references inference.sh documentation, a hosted image, and placeholder HTTPS media URLs. These URLs are not evidence of exfiltration, but users should know avatar images, audio, video, and scripts may be sent to external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in documentation and command inputs. They support the advertised workflow and do not show hidden or suspicious endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged SKILL.md line 3 as weak cryptography, but that line is only the skill description metadata. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic implementation in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains descriptive text about avatar video capabilities, not cryptographic code. The skill file contains no implementation that performs cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Permission for belt CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The frontmatter allows Bash commands matching belt. This narrows the allowed command surface, but belt remains an external networked CLI that can process user-provided media.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit. The risk is bounded to belt usage and does not grant unrestricted Bash access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Media Processing Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflows pass portrait, audio, video, and script inputs to hosted inference apps. This creates privacy and consent risk when users process real people, voices, or unpublished content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly send media URLs and text prompts to remote apps. This is normal for the skill but requires user awareness about data handling.

content.value_statement: Avatar video production can require many separate media tools and model choices. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh workflows for talking head videos.
actual_capabilities: Selects between P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse lip sync workflows.
Shows how to create avatar videos from a portrait and text script.
Shows how to create avatar videos from a portrait and audio file.
Combines image generation, speech generation, transcription, translation, and lip sync steps.
Compares model speed, cost, TTS support, and best-fit use cases.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an authenticated account.
Remote inference may process user images, audio, video, and scripts.
Generated video quality depends on source media, model limits, and prompt clarity.
The skill does not verify consent, likeness rights, or disclosure requirements.

use_cases.title: Build Product Demo Presenters
use_cases.description: Create a virtual presenter from a portrait and short product script for demos or announcements.

use_cases.title: Localize Existing Videos
use_cases.description: Transcribe, translate, generate new speech, and lip sync a video into another language.

use_cases.title: Prototype Character Dialogue
use_cases.description: Generate quick talking character clips for game, course, or social media concepts.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Presenter
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner avatar video from a portrait and script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking head video from this portrait URL and script. Use the fastest suitable inference.sh avatar workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Avatar Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose the right model before generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the available avatar video models for my use case. Consider cost, speed, TTS support, and output quality.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Full Avatar Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Create portrait, speech, and video assets together
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a complete workflow to generate a portrait, create narration, and produce a vertical avatar video.

prompt_templates.title: Localize and Lip Sync a Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow to transcribe this video, translate the script, generate new speech, and lip sync the original speaker.

output_examples.input: A portrait URL and a 30-second product announcement script.
output_examples.output: A recommended P-Video-Avatar workflow with voice choice, resolution guidance, and the expected avatar video result.

output_examples.input: A training video that needs a Spanish dubbed version.
output_examples.output: A staged plan covering transcription, translation, speech generation, lip sync, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A request to choose between OmniHuman, Fabric, PixVerse, and P-Video-Avatar.
output_examples.output: A concise model recommendation based on number of people, audio source, TTS needs, speed, and cost.

best_practices: Use clear consented portraits, clean audio, and scripts that match the speaker identity.
Start with P-Video-Avatar unless multi-character control or a special lip sync model is needed.
Review generated videos for likeness accuracy, disclosure needs, and brand safety before publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not upload private faces, voices, or videos without consent and policy review.
Do not use low-resolution portraits or noisy speech for final production outputs.
Do not publish synthetic presenter videos without checking local disclosure rules.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create AI avatar, talking head, lip sync, and dubbed videos through inference.sh CLI workflows.

faq.question: Which model should I start with?
faq.answer: Start with P-Video-Avatar when you need a fast avatar video with built-in text-to-speech.

faq.question: Can it use my own audio?
faq.answer: Yes. Several workflows accept an audio URL to drive mouth movement and speech timing.

faq.question: Can it create the presenter image too?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a workflow that generates a portrait first, then uses it for avatar video.

faq.question: Does this run locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI and hosted inference apps, so media may be processed remotely.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing output?
faq.answer: Check consent, likeness rights, disclosure requirements, voice accuracy, and final video quality.
